title: The Obvious Answer To America’s Vanishing Babies | Institute for Family Studies
gist: Demographer Wendy R. Wang, writing in the Daily Wire, argues the U.S. fertility decline is not the mystery Wall Street Journal columnist Louise Perry calls it: the data point to the drop in the number of children women have in their 20s as the direct cause, not the falling teen birth rate that the New York Times' Jessica Grose credits.

insights.claim: Wendy R. Wang argues the U.S. fertility decline is not a mystery: the data show the direct cause is a massive drop in the number of children women have during their 20s, not falling teen birth rates.
insights.speaker: Wendy R. Wang
insights.so_what: Reframes the pronatalism debate around childbearing in women's 20s rather than teen pregnancy trends, pointing any family-formation policy or intervention toward the actual driver of the decline.
insights.quote: The data clearly point to the massive drop in the number of children women have during their 20s as the direct cause.

insights.claim: Wall Street Journal columnist Louise Perry and New York Times columnist Jessica Grose publicly disagreed on the same day about whether the cause of falling U.S. birth rates is a mystery, with Grose attributing the decline to the drop in teen births.
insights.speaker: Wendy R. Wang
insights.so_what: Shows even elite mainstream commentary is unsettled on fertility-decline causation, leaving room for a demographer's counter-narrative (20s childbearing) to gain traction in the policy debate.
insights.quote: Falling birth rates are a mystery
